message.content:
Adversaries may use external proxies to act as intermediaries for network communications to command and control (C2) servers, thereby avoiding direct connections to their infrastructure. This technique helps mask the destination of C2 traffic, providing resiliency and leveraging trusted communication paths to evade detection. Tools like HTRAN, ZXProxy, and ZXPortMap facilitate traffic redirection through proxies or port redirection. Adversaries may use compromised systems, cloud-based resources, or virtual private servers as proxies. Notable groups such as APT28, APT3, APT39, FIN5, GALLIUM, Lazarus Group, and others have employed this method to obscure their activities. The impact includes difficulty in tracing the origin of malicious traffic and potential prolonged access to compromised environments.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring for unusual outbound traffic patterns, implementing network segmentation, using intrusion detection systems (IDS) to identify proxy-related anomalies, and employing strict egress filtering to control outbound traffic. Regularly updating threat intelligence and employing behavioral analytics can also help detect and mitigate the use of external proxies.
